story:
Dolly Jameson, fifty-two and fond of order, lives by the comfort of small certainties: her alarm sings at 6:15 a.m., she stretches, prays, brews tea, and hums while ZoZo—fat, grey, and judgmental—watches from the stairs as if he runs the place. But one Tuesday, the alarm fails. She wakes at 7:02, trips over ZoZo’s slow-motion descent, misses the bus, and is baptized by five separate car splashes. Soggy and late, she reaches the office only to learn she’s first up to present the quarterly report. In the restroom, her reflection blinks out of sync—and smiles without her. She shuts her eyes in panic.

She opens them in bed. The alarm chimes 6:15. Rain is gone. ZoZo licks a paw. A dream, she thinks—until ZoZo says, clear as speech, “Don’t miss your bus, Dolly.” She drops her teacup. He urges her to hurry. Outside, everything happens again: the rain, the five splashes, the old man who says, “You again?” At work, she races to the mirror. It speaks. “I’m you. Or you’re me. Close your eyes.” She does.

Reset. The alarm flickers—6:15, then 7:02, then nonsense. “Which one do you believe?” ZoZo purrs. Downstairs, her lunch simmers on a low burner, the rice scorched—left there “yesterday” on a day that might be today. Her phone says Wednesday. ZoZo muses, “Time is flexible when you don’t believe in it.” Mr. Banda calls; the shareholders are waiting. In a blink, she’s at the projector, slides glowing, throat stuffed with cotton. The entire room turns, in eerie unison, toward the window as a bus slashes rain across the glass. ZoZo’s voice drifts: “You missed the bus again.” Dolly screams. Darkness.

She wakes in a hospital bed—tubes, beeping, a nurse adjusting her blanket. “You fainted at work,” the nurse says. “Two days asleep.” Relief, until Dolly reads the name tag: Zozo. Rain begins outside the window.

Two weeks later, she stitches routine back together—tea, rest, ZoZo’s fur under her brush—and tells herself it was overwork and stress. But sometimes she hears him murmur, “Again.” One morning, the alarm rings right on time. No ZoZo at the bed. He’s on the stairs, descending with ceremonial slowness. The clock insists it’s 7:02. Rain starts inside the bedroom. A horn. SPLASH. Dolly stands drenched beneath a leaking ceiling. “You’re late, Dolly,” ZoZo says.

She laughs—wild, tired. Downstairs, every surface is a mirror, each reflecting a different Dolly: one crying, one poised at a podium, one calmly brushing her hair. “Which one is real?” she asks. “All of them. None of them. You decide,” ZoZo replies. “I want to wake up.” “Then sleep.” “And if I’m asleep?” “Then dream better.”

Later—if “later” exists—her neighbor knocks. No answer. The landlord opens a spotless house: a teacup on the table, lunch neatly packed on the stove, an alarm frozen at 6:15. ZoZo sits on the stairs, eyes bright, tail ticking like a second hand. He looks up and, almost amused, says, “She finally caught the bus.”
